About me

Design for the person,
not the screen.

Case studies

I'm a UX/UI and product designer with 5+ years of experience across infrastructure, government, fintech, and insurance - designing systems that real people depend on every day.

I design around outcomes, not deliverables. That means starting with the person using the system - understanding their context, their pressures, their workarounds - before touching a frame.

I'm returning to full-time design and open to roles across UX, UI, product design, and interaction design - wherever the problems are hard and the user impact is real.

Selected work

Case studies

Case study 01
Integra SCADA HMI Redesign
UGL · Product Designer · Desktop + Mobile · 2022-2025
Safety-criticalInfrastructureDesktop + Mobile
Opening

When an infrastructure operator misses a critical alarm, the consequences aren't a bad user experience - they're a service failure, a safety risk, or worse. By the time we completed the redesign, system testing showed a 25% improvement in emergency response time - the result of designing every screen around one question: what does an operator need to stay in control under pressure?

The problem

Operators worked in 24/7 control rooms with a legacy interface causing visual inconsistency, unreliable alarm hierarchy, and eye strain across long shifts. We observed operators scanning through dozens of simultaneous alarms - unable to quickly identify which required immediate action.

"When something goes wrong, everything goes red. I can't tell what's actually on fire."

- Operator, contextual inquiry
Key design decisions
01 · Dark theme - sustained attention, not preference

Operators work in low-ambient control rooms across 8-12 hour shifts. A dark theme was an environmental decision, validated against WCAG 2.1 AA. Consistency across all operators matters more than personal preference in a safety-critical environment.

02 · Three-tier alarm prioritisation

We restructured alarms around Critical, Elevated, and Informational - using visual weight rather than colour alone. Critical alarms occupy a persistent top zone. Priority-first matched how operators think in an emergency: severity first, then location.

03 · Modular components - consistency as a safety feature

A shared Figma component library with strict behavioural rules. Any component responds identically across every screen. Consistency here isn't a design preference - it's a safety consideration.

Outcomes
25%
Faster emergency response
WCAG AA
Accessibility compliance
1 library
Unified component system
Reflection

Involve operators earlier - I'd have fought harder for that access from day one. Good design in complex environments is as much about what you remove as what you add.

Selected screens

A sample of the SCADA interface patterns, alarm hierarchy components, settings screens and design-system foundations used across the redesigned operator experience.

Case study 02
Performance Management System
Transport for NSW · Sole Lead UX Designer · Web + Mobile · 2023-2025
Government0→1 ProductCross-platformEnterprise
Opening

Managing Sydney's train fleet against service contracts worth millions. The platform reduced reporting time by 50%, eliminated manual reconciliation across three teams, and gave operations controllers live fleet visibility for the first time. Sole lead designer - 13+ screen types across web and mobile.

The problem

Three teams managing train performance across disconnected spreadsheets. Controllers worked from hours-old data. Maintenance logs got lost in handovers. Finance reconciled mismatched records after the fact.

"We're making decisions about the fleet based on yesterday's data. By the time the spreadsheet gets updated, the situation has already changed."

- Operations controller, synthesised from stakeholder sessions
Key design decisions
01 · One system, two completely different contexts

Maintenance staff in depots needed to log a fault fast, one hand free - a categorically different context from an operations manager analysing fleet trends. Mobile was designed around the smallest unit of work; desktop around density and insight.

02 · Information hierarchy across 13+ screen types

Status before detail, exceptions before routine, actions before information. Operations sees live status. Management sees trends. Finance sees payment-relevant data. Same source, different lens.

Outcomes
50%
Reduction in reporting time
3
User groups unified
13+
Screen types delivered
Reflection

Making consequential decisions as the sole designer taught me to write the why before the what. With one more voice pressure-testing the work, it could have been even better.

Selected screens

A sample of the PMS screens covering scheduling, payment reporting, unit unavailability and operational workflows across desktop and mobile contexts.

Case study 03
Insurance Management App
Confidential Client · UX Designer · Web + Android
InsuranceFintechOnboardingAndroid + Web
Opening

A fully managed insurance advisory service needed a product that felt as trustworthy and simple as the service behind it. My focus was the onboarding, contract management and document-signing experience - the make-or-break moment where a new user decides whether to trust the product with sensitive insurance and financial information.

The problem

The information users need to provide for personalised insurance advice is often the same information they are most hesitant to share. The experience needed to support different levels of trust, explain why information was needed, and help users continue across web and mobile without feeling lost.

"I don't understand what I'm signing. I just click through and hope for the best."

- Insurance app user, representative sentiment
Key design decisions
01 · Two-phase registration

Phase 1 framed around access. Phase 2 framed around value. Users who completed Phase 1 were already invested; the second phase followed naturally. Existing data pre-filled wherever possible.

02 · Document signing - transparent, not bureaucratic

Plain-language explanation before every signature. Drawn signatures for formal legal documents, toggle for lower-stakes confirmations. A user who signs because they understand - not because they gave up.

03 · Consistent web and mobile experience

A shared design system kept the web and mobile experience consistent, while interaction patterns were adjusted for each context - dense contract management on web and focused step-by-step tasks on mobile.

Outcomes
2
Web and mobile journeys designed
2-phase
Onboarding reducing drop-off
10+
Service types supported
Reflection

The strongest design decision was slowing users down at the right moments. For this product, trust mattered more than speed alone - the experience needed to help people understand what they were doing before asking them to continue.

Selected screens

A sample of the refreshed, more natural-looking insurance app screens, showing the dashboard, account setup, contract management and service support journeys.

How I work

My design process

01
Start with people

Before any wireframe, I need to understand who's using the system, under what conditions, and what's genuinely hard for them. Research informs every decision.

02
Design with reasoning

Every decision has a why. I document alternatives considered and the reason for the choice - to keep my thinking honest and handoffs clear.

03
Test and iterate

Assumptions get challenged early. Usability testing or stakeholder review - I'd rather find the friction in a prototype than in a live system.

04
Measure outcomes

Design is only as good as the outcome it produces. I connect decisions to results - for users and the business - not just deliverables.

Let's solve something worth solving.

I'm open to all design roles - UX, UI, product, interaction. If you're building something complex and your users deserve better - I'd love to help you get there.
